title: Sri Lankan Chicken Curry

ingredients.name: chicken, curry cut with bone
visual_checks: Bone-in pieces give the best flavour; whole thighs and drumsticks work well.
ingredients.notes: Bone-in pieces give the best flavour; whole thighs and drumsticks work well.

subs.name: 600 g bone-in mutton pieces — use the same method but increase simmering time to 40 minutes or pressure-cook for 5 whistles on medium flame.

ingredients.name: Sri Lankan roasted curry powder
visual_checks: Use the dark-roasted variety; available at South Asian grocery stores as Ceylon curry powder. See substitutions to make your own.
ingredients.notes: Use the dark-roasted variety; available at South Asian grocery stores as Ceylon curry powder. See substitutions to make your own.

subs.name: Homemade roasted curry powder: dry-roast 2 tbsp coriander seeds, 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp fennel, and 6 dried red chillies in a pan until dark and fragrant, then cool and grind fine.

ingredients.name: onion, thinly sliced
visual_checks: Red or yellow; shallots give a sweeter, more authentic result.
ingredients.notes: Red or yellow; shallots give a sweeter, more authentic result.

ingredients.name: lemongrass stalk, bruised
visual_checks: Bash with the knife handle to release the oils; remove before serving.
ingredients.notes: Bash with the knife handle to release the oils; remove before serving.

subs.name: One extra sprig of fresh curry leaves plus a 1-inch strip of lemon zest added during simmering — different but aromatic.

ingredients.name: green chillies, slit lengthwise
visual_checks: Reduce to 1 for a milder curry.
ingredients.notes: Reduce to 1 for a milder curry.

ingredients.name: pandan leaf (rampe)
visual_checks: Pandan adds a subtle floral note; kaffir lime leaf is the closest available substitute in Indian markets.
ingredients.notes: Pandan adds a subtle floral note; kaffir lime leaf is the closest available substitute in Indian markets.

ingredients.name: coconut milk, full-fat
visual_checks: Thick coconut milk from a can or packet; full-fat gives the best body to the gravy.
ingredients.notes: Thick coconut milk from a can or packet; full-fat gives the best body to the gravy.

subs.name: 3 tbsp coconut milk powder dissolved in 250 ml warm water; the flavour is slightly lighter but works well.

ingredients.name: cooking oil
visual_checks: Coconut oil is most authentic; any neutral cooking oil works.
ingredients.notes: Coconut oil is most authentic; any neutral cooking oil works.

steps.text: Toss the chicken pieces with 1½ tbsp of the roasted curry powder, the turmeric, and ½ tsp salt. Turn each piece so it is well coated. Set aside while you prepare the aromatics.

Heat the oil in a heavy-bottomed pot or kadai over medium-high flame. Add the sliced onions and fry, stirring regularly, until they turn a deep golden brown — about 10 minutes. This caramelisation is what creates the dark, complex base characteristic of Sri Lankan curry.

Add the garlic, ginger, lemongrass, pandan leaf (if using), curry leaves, and slit green chillies. Stir and fry on medium heat for 2 minutes until the kitchen is filled with fragrance. Do not let the garlic burn.
Stir in the remaining ½ tbsp curry powder and the chopped tomato. Cook for 3 minutes, pressing the tomato pieces against the pot until they completely soften and merge into the masala base.

Add the marinated chicken and increase heat to high. Stir to coat every piece in the masala and fry for 5 minutes, turning the chicken occasionally, until it browns slightly on the outside and the raw pink colour disappears.

Pour in the coconut milk and 100 ml water. Stir, bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low. Cover and simmer until the chicken is cooked through and oil begins to rise to the surface — about 20 minutes. Taste and adjust salt.
Remove the lemongrass stalk and pandan leaf. Garnish with fresh coriander leaves and serve hot with steamed rice, hoppers, or string hoppers.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: curry-powder → Homemade roasted curry powder: dry-roast 2 tbsp coriander seeds, 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp fennel, and 6 dried red chillies in a pan until dark and fragrant, then cool and grind fine.; coconut-milk → 3 tbsp coconut milk powder dissolved in 250 ml warm water; the flavour is slightly lighter but works well.; lemongrass → One extra sprig of fresh curry leaves plus a 1-inch strip of lemon zest added during simmering — different but aromatic.; chicken → 600 g bone-in mutton pieces — use the same method but increase simmering time to 40 minutes or pressure-cook for 5 whistles on medium flame.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook in a thick-bottomed kadai or pressure cooker (4 whistles on medium flame, then open-simmer for 5 minutes to thicken the gravy). Sri Lankan roasted curry powder is sold as 'Ceylon curry powder' at South Indian and Sri Lankan grocery stores; substitute with a blend of 1 tbsp meat masala and 1 tsp dark-roasted coriander powder if unavailable. Pandan leaf can be skipped or replaced with a fresh curry leaf sprig. Any Indian branded coconut milk packet works perfectly.
